hook: The only Warsaw synagogue to survive WWII — the Nazis used it as a horse stable.
facts: Founded by Zalman and Rywka (Ryfka) Nożyk; built 1898–1902, opened 1902.
The only pre-war Warsaw synagogue to survive the war; its solid construction did not collapse despite Uprising street-fighting and bombing.
During the occupation it was turned into stables for horses and a fodder store; briefly reopened for worship in 1941, then closed again in July 1942 during the great deportation action.
Still an active synagogue and the religious centre of Warsaw's Jewish community, at ul. Twarda 6 / pl. Grzybowski.
Restoration began in 1945; the building is often attributed to architect Leandro Marconi.

sources.note: Polish Wikipedia — history, WWII survival, stables, coordinates 52.236, 21.0011
Wirtualny Sztetl — founding, address ul. Twarda 6, history

w: The only synagogue in Warsaw to come through the war — built by the Nożyks at the turn of the century, too solidly to be brought down by the fighting around it. The Germans stabled their horses inside and stored fodder; worshippers came back in 1945. Still the heart of Jewish Warsaw, off plac Grzybowski. One survivor among the lost.

hook: A surviving stretch of the Warsaw Ghetto wall, hidden in a courtyard off Sienna and Złota.
facts: One of the best-known surviving fragments of the Warsaw Ghetto wall, in the courtyards between Sienna 55 and Złota 62.
The Sienna 55 wall (about 3 m high) predated the ghetto and marked the boundary between the ghetto and the 'Aryan' side after 1940.
Two original bricks from the Sienna 55 wall were taken to the U.S. Holocaust Memorial Museum in Washington in 1989.
Bricks from the Złota 62 fragment (about 6 m high) reached Yad Vashem in Jerusalem and museums in Houston and Melbourne.
A commemorative plaque marks the western side of the surviving wall.

sources.note: Polish Wikipedia — Sienna 55 / Złota 62 fragment, heights, museum bricks, coordinates 52.231014, 20.998439
Warszawy historia ukryta — detailed account of the Sienna 55 / Złota 62 wall

w: A stretch of the Warsaw Ghetto wall still standing, hidden in a courtyard between Sienna and Złota — three metres of brick that split the ghetto from the 'Aryan' side. Bricks from it sit in Holocaust museums in Washington, Jerusalem and beyond. A plaque, a passage, and a line that once meant life or death.

hook: Warsaw's first skyscraper and subject of the most iconic Uprising photograph.
facts: Built 1931–1933 (opened 1934) for the British Prudential insurance company at the former plac Napoleona (now pl. Powstańców Warszawy 9), designed by Marcin Weinfeld in an art déco style.
At 66 m (16 storeys) it was the tallest building in Poland and the second-tallest in Europe at the time; steel structure engineered by Stefan Bryła and Wenczesław Poniż.
In 1936 Poland's first television transmitter was installed on its roof.

During the 1944 Uprising it was struck by a heavy projectile from a German self-propelled mortar; photographer Sylwester Braun 'Kris' captured the explosion from a nearby rooftop on 28 August — among the most recognisable images of the Uprising.
Reopened as the luxury Hotel Warszawa after reconstruction.

sources.note: Polish Wikipedia — Weinfeld, 66 m, Braun 'Kris' Uprising photo, coordinates 52.235556, 21.012778
Architektura-Murator — tallest pre-war Warsaw building, art déco, history

w: Warsaw's first skyscraper — sixteen art déco storeys built for the British Prudential in 1934, the tallest in Poland at the time, with the country's first TV transmitter on its roof. A giant German shell struck it in 1944; Sylwester Braun caught the blast in one of the Uprising's most famous photos. It took the hit and stood.

hook: Europe's pioneering early skyscraper, stormed by the insurgents in one of the Uprising's greatest victories.
facts: Built 1908–1910 for the Swedish 'Cedergren' telephone company; designed by Bronisław Brochwicz-Rogóyski as one of Europe's first reinforced-concrete high-rises, modelled on the Chicago School.
At 51.5 m it was Warsaw's tallest building until the Prudential was completed in 1934; from 1922 it housed the Polska Akcyjna Spółka Telefoniczna (PAST).
Insurgent battles to capture the German-held building lasted from 2 to 20 August 1944.
On the night of 19–20 August 1944 the Kiliński battalion pumped a burning mixture of oil and petrol into the building; the German garrison surrendered the morning of 20 August.
Its capture, one of the greatest successes of the Warsaw Uprising, is commemorated each year; the building now houses the Polish Underground State Foundation.

sources.note: Polish Wikipedia — 1908–1910, 51.5 m, first skyscraper, 20 Aug 1944 capture, coordinates 52.236111, 21.006389
Fundacja Warszawa 1939 — Cedergren/PAST, tallest in Russian Empire, Uprising siege

w: One of Europe's first concrete high-rises, built in 1910 for a Swedish phone company on the Chicago model — and the scene of one of the Uprising's great victories. Insurgents fought the German garrison inside for eighteen days in 1944, until the Kiliński battalion pumped burning petrol in and forced its surrender. The telephone tower, taken.

hook: Romanowicz's 1975 modernist station, built at breakneck speed for a Brezhnev visit.
facts: Opened 5 December 1975, designed by Arseniusz Romanowicz (with Piotr Szymaniak); Romanowicz had won the design competition back in 1946.
Defined by a massive canopy on slender reinforced-concrete columns and a double glazed curtain wall.
Built urgently for a planned visit by Soviet leader Leonid Brezhnev, who attended the opening; concrete was poured even at minus 7°C with army help.
Won the 'Mister Warszawy 1975' title; called by critic Werner Huber 'a pearl on rails' and considered the most modern station in Europe in the 1970s.
Located at Al. Jerozolimskie 54; later named after composer Stanisław Moniuszko.

sources.note: Polish Wikipedia — Romanowicz, 1975, Brezhnev deadline, coordinates 52.228778, 21.003333
Architektura-Murator — Romanowicz interview, Brezhnev rush, minus 7°C concrete

w: Warsaw's modernist central station, thrown up at frantic speed in 1975 for a Brezhnev visit — concrete poured by soldiers in hard frost to be ready in time. Arseniusz Romanowicz had won the competition for it back in 1946; he got a vast canopy on slim columns and walls of glass. The most modern station in 1970s Europe.

hook: The sword-raising Warsaw Mermaid on the Vistula bank, modelled on a poet killed in the Uprising.
facts: Bronze Mermaid by sculptor Ludwika Nitschowa, unveiled in 1939 on the Vistula embankment (Wybrzeże Kościuszkowskie), near the later Świętokrzyski bridge.
The 2.75 m figure holds a raised sword and a shield bearing an eagle; cast in 1938 at the Łopieński Brothers foundry on ul. Hoża 55.
Ethnography student Krystyna Krahelska posed kneeling for the sculptor, sword in hand; Krahelska later died of wounds as a medic in the 1944 Uprising.
The Mermaid (Syrenka) is the heraldic symbol of Warsaw; this riverside version is distinct from the Old Town Market Square statue.
legend: The Warsaw Mermaid of legend was a sister of the Copenhagen mermaid who swam up the Vistula and chose to stay, becoming the city's guardian.

sources.note: Polish Wikipedia — Nitschowa, 1939, Krahelska model, coordinates 52.24, 21.031389
Muzeum Warszawy — Mermaid monument, casting details, dimensions

w: The Warsaw Mermaid on the Vistula bank, sword raised over a shield, cast in bronze in 1939 — the city's heraldic guardian, and not the little one on the Old Town square. Ludwika Nitschowa modelled her on the poet Krystyna Krahelska, who would die as a medic in the 1944 Uprising. A symbol, with a tragic face.

hook: A near-untouched 18th-century royal court theatre inside the Łazienki Orangery.
facts: Court theatre built 1786–1788 by architect Dominik Merlini in the eastern wing of King Stanisław August's Old Orangery (Stara Pomarańczarnia); opened 6 September 1788.
Seats about 200, with nine boxes on three walls and original decoration including a royal coat of arms and medallions of Sophocles, Shakespeare, Molière and Racine.
Jan Bogumił Plersch painted illusionistic murals of further box seating filled with a courtly audience.
Survived World War II intact, making it one of very few original 18th-century court theatres preserved in Europe; described as 'unique on a worldwide scale'.
A museum venue since 1962 and home stage of the Polish Royal Opera since 2017.

sources.note: Polish Wikipedia — Merlini, 1786–1788, intact through WWII, unique court theatre, coordinates 52.21648, 21.03034
Łazienki Królewskie official site — Royal Theatre and Old Orangery

w: A royal court theatre of 1788, hidden in the Old Orangery of Łazienki and barely touched in two centuries — 200 seats, nine boxes, medallions of Sophocles, Shakespeare and Molière on the walls. Plersch painted a phantom audience into the upper boxes, watching forever. It came through the war intact, one of the last of its kind in Europe.

hook: The secret burial pit where the communist regime dumped executed 'Cursed Soldiers' — now a memorial.
facts: Quarter 'Ł' ('Łączka') at the edge of the Military Powązki Cemetery (Cmentarz Wojskowy na Powązkach, established 1912), distinct from the main and Jewish Powązki cemeteries.

Between 1948 and the mid-1950s the security service (UB) secretly buried here victims of Stalinist repression, many shot in Mokotów (Rakowiecka) prison; 1955–56 the quarter was covered with over a metre of earth.
Among the murdered buried here were Home Army officers, NSZ and WiN soldiers, cichociemni and Uprising insurgents holding the Virtuti Militari.
IPN exhumations from 2012 recovered and identified remains including Hieronim Dekutowski 'Zapora' and Zygmunt Szendzielarz 'Łupaszka'.
The Panteon–Mauzoleum Wyklętych-Niezłomnych (designers Jan Kuka and Michał Dąbek), with 300 burial spaces, was unveiled on 27 September 2015.

sources.note: Polish Wikipedia — 2015 Panteon, designers, 300 spaces, identified dead, coordinates 52.255833, 20.952361
IPN Przystanek Historia — secret UB burials 1948–55, exhumations on the Łączka

w: A patch of grass at the edge of the Military Powązki, and one of the regime's darkest secrets — from 1948 the secret police dumped executed resistance fighters here, then buried the pit under a metre of earth. From 2012 forensic teams dug them up and named them: 'Łupaszka,' 'Zapora,' and more. A mausoleum stands over them now.
